speaker: interviewer
text: 23Rd of July 1995. And we're in Sydney, Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: John, did you see your mother again after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't see her.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't see her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your aunt?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never knew that my aunt is there. She just got that few words by coincidence.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, really? Can you tell me about the time that you spent in Birkenau? How-- how long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, approximately 10 days.

speaker: interviewer
text: 10 Days?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were-- you're now about, what, you must be May 1944--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --At that time. OK. Can you tell me of your experiences in Birkenau?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well after arrival and selection, we went off. We walked about 10 minutes to a-- to a center, which-- which we had to discard all our clothing in a yard. And I remember we-- we could keep shoes and belts around. But I forgot my belt on-- on my pants. And they wouldn't let me go back to the heap, for the huge heap already was three, four meter high, full of pants and jackets. And they wouldn't let me there. So I found a pair of tefillin And I cut the leather off and use it for a belt for a few weeks, until I got a belt in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would your pants not stay up without the belt?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, those prison-- they were giving us striped prison pants. And they--

speaker: interviewer
text: But they didn't have elastic or anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Which-- well, you couldn't choose. Now after that, they were shearing all the hair off everybody. They were prisoners, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: The prisoners were-- were shaving your hair off.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Prisoners who are working. That was very proximity of the crematorium. And they-- we didn't know all these details. But some of them were-- were joking, telling, you don't worry-- you go in through the door, you come out through the chimney. And they were laughing. But they knew that--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you believe them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they knew we were sent to work. But they were telling you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they give you a number in Birkenau?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. And, well, after I got my shoes and my improvised belt, and we went to have a shower. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we had to submerge the shoes or belts in a carbolic solution, little cement bars. And some of the staffs, there were girls there working. That was actually a Jewish girl, a Polish Jewish girl, who had a sort of a sponge with a solution. And they put on everyone's head to disinfect his head, as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your head was-- head was already shaven?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shaven. And then we had a shower, a hot shower with some soap in there. And we have soap. And after the shower, they handed also prisoners, they handed this prison clothing. But we didn't have enough time. So told us we were given short sleeves and-- and short pants, and we could change with another one if we were lucky. If not, you just have to bear it. And we were taken to the gypsy camp for the night.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, that's in the barracks, but the gypsies were in charge?

speaker: interviewee
text: The gypsies were in charge, yeah. And those gypsies were-- were with their families. Each family had a little room in the barrack. Barrack accommodated about between 600 and 800 people. They were horse barracks from the First World War, concreted. And they even had the heating facilities for the horses, very long brick meter high in the middle, which were actually fired two hands with wood or coal, and they had two little four-inch chimneys going out from every barrack.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did that-- did it warm the barracks for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They warmed-- in the First World War, the horses were warmed. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: Warmed the horses, but didn't warm the Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were not-- it wasn't cold at that time anyway. But the gypsies came middle of the night. He couldn't sleep. Some people wanted to go to toilets, or-- and they only took a lot of people to the toilet, there were 20 together. And the toilets were a separate barrack, which-- which was long, cement slabs, which had about half inch steel [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in about 10 inch deep from the cement level. So nobody can jump and escape through. Down was a real dam that could have been six, or eight-foot diameter with cement, where the water flew like a river permanently.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. So-- so it would wash away.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything washed away. But that water could have been two foot deep as you looked in it. But to get down there, you-- you would have had to cut heavy steel in every toilet hole. And so when the gypsies couldn't sleep, they came out and beat some of the fellows up with long sticks. And he was working on that brick-built stove in the middle, which was about two and a half foot from the ground high. And people were sleeping inside each other in a row on the cement. It was hard to get out, really, from there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why were you sleeping like that, fitted into each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: You could sit on the next person was sleeping between your knees.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there no bunks for you to sleep on?

speaker: interviewee
text: No bunks there. And these bunks, you-- you wouldn't put more than 200 people in that. They would pack 600 to 800 in it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you could never stretch out there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never stretch out in that place. Some fellows went up on the crossbeam, and they tied themselves with their belts so-- so that when they fall asleep, so they won't fall down.

speaker: interviewer
text: To the rafters?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the rafters, yeah. There was feet hanging, with-- with a hand tied to it. You would see them sleeping like that. Food was very scarce. They-- in the morning, they gave you-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] some they called it tea, some dark, dark water. But it was very hot, warm. And the dishes they requisitioned from the railways which came-- people brought different sized dishes. Two three, up to 10 liters, some of them. And they used to fill it and give it to groups. And the groups had to-- same for-- for dinner or lunch, whatever soup they gave. And groups, to make it equitable, they were counting your Adam--

speaker: interviewer
text: Adam's apple.

speaker: interviewee
text: --apple. How many times you swallow. Five times each. Had to go five times until the thing is finished if they had a two or five liter dish with food.

speaker: interviewer
text: To be sure that nobody took more than--

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody took extra.

speaker: interviewer
text: Besides the-- besides the tea, did you get any other food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We get zuppa. They call it bunker soup. That's a-- a semolina soup with bits of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] vegetables-- some dry carrots or sugar beet, dry sugar-- after the sugar is extracted from the sugar beet, the left is given-- they feed animals with it. So they put that in. And-- and we got a slice of bread sometimes in the evening.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just once a day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just one slice, yeah. And they-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that bunker zuppa had some bits of meat. You didn't know what meat it was, but it was very watery, the whole thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Weren't people beginning to get to weak at that stage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They-- they-- they-- many got weak. And I saw-- I saw people shaving with finding a spoon. Well, nobody had a spoon, but some were brought in somehow. And they were not used to eat. The handle was sharpened on the cement so they could shave with it. And the one was showing the other. Some-- most of them wouldn't shave at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I met a Polish man who was transferred from Dachau to Birkenau. And he told me that-- this was after 10 days, roughly-- that tomorrow morning the transport goes to Dachau. If you want to go, he was in that area. It's-- It's not too bad. You have to work there, that's all. They won't kill you like here. And you've got to be between the first two barracks, because I was in number 17 in-- in Birkenau. And your father was still with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: With me. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] every-- every morning and evening, they made appeals, the Germans. And it took about 40, 45 minutes before they counted so many barracks. And we were standing outside, and two Germans came in with one prisoner local, and he went with the three like a group. And they wrote down barrack number 17, whatever, 600 people, and counted them. No one really could escape anymore here. There were electrified wires around. And between the two camps were also electrified the wire. Before the electric wire, you had--

speaker: interviewer
text: The two camps, you mean between Birkenau and Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I mean Birkenau itself was divided where-- where these children were, and then the women's camp. And between those camps, also electric wire were running. You couldn't get through. And you couldn't not only handed-- you couldn't handed a letter over. You had to throw it with a stone attached to it, because the electrified fence had a small warning fence, which-- which was only about 4-foot high, of wire. But you could go through that. That was only-- the wire to carry the warning, maybe, a little plate, 6,000 volts or whatever it was. And on the other side of the fence was the same. So you had actually three fences. Well, I-- I thought-- by that time,  you knew what's going on in the place. Because we saw the crematoriums.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they do with you all day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing in Birkenau. We were just sitting, you know, go in the yard, and twice appear. And actually, I met a-- a cousin who was from [NON-ENGLISH], and told him about what the Polish man told us. And we want to be in that transport if we can. So I'm leaving the barrack, as all get up very early before the counting, and moving into the first row of the first barrack. Because if they cut off 1,500, and that's it. That's what happened. And nobody knew which barrack you are, because we were all in these pajamas.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't have names. They just wanted the right number.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Nobody had names there. No numbers, no names. And they wanted 1,500, so they got 1,500. And I just told my father, he saw the same, and it can't be worse than here. You do nothing, no food. And you see, you-- you can see the crematoriums. And these people in charge, they already knew. They told us what's going on. So that known very quickly after we arrived.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the-- what did they tell you? Did they tell you that the crematoriums, they were taking people alive, or they were taking people who'd died?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They-- they're telling us there's gas chambers there. And they--

speaker: interviewer
text: Gas chambers and--

speaker: interviewee
text: And crematorium. They gassing the people and they burning them. And that-- that's-- after ten days you knew every detail. You even knew-- knew that those people who were working and distributing stuff for us, they also staff of the crematorium. And after three months they were put in themselves. They had a good life for three months. They were given extra food and privileges. But three months, they were killed. And another staff took over, which also got killed. Apparently the last lot might have survived part of it, when the Russians run-- run over.

speaker: interviewer
text: John, did you see any atrocities in the time that you were there? Any beatings?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Birkenau, only beating by these gypsies.

speaker: interviewer
text: The German guards themselves didn't beat the people?

speaker: interviewee
text: The German guard only came in when they counted us. But we saw people who died by committing suicide. They went through the first little fence, and they touching the wires.

speaker: interviewer
text: On purpose?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were collected on stretchers and taken out of the camp by other prisoners. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] That's-- they knew when they-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when we were cut off next morning, the 1,500, we was taken out to the further out of camp. That means the square. See, the camp was divided a long way, different camps, maybe five or six different camps. We were not in the last one, but the one next. That was the Zigeurnerlager.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the 1,500 were taken out and walked-- walked over to this camp. That's called F Lager, which was a sort of a transit. And also some barracks were used for people who were nearly dying. They couldn't stand on their feet. We saw two barracks in the morning, which these people pulled out the bodies. And they were taking them like timber on-- stacked up. On little cots?

speaker: interviewee
text: On cots, yeah. And then they put them on cots with rubber wheels pushed by prisoners, taken out from the camp. But they died from weakness.

speaker: interviewer
text: Starvation.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were in that camp only two days in that F Lager. And then we were given a half a loaf of bread, and a piece of margarina, and-- and a blanket.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. When you lived the Zigeurnerlager-- that's the gypsy lager-- and you went into F Lager, didn't they miss you? Didn't they notice that you were missing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well in the gypsy lager, no. How could they miss 1,500 when every day they sending transports all over the place into Germany into different Polish places? A new arrival coming everyday. Nobody knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you-- you said they used to come in and count you every day.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well the Germans took us over.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, they themselves took you?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was a transport which they send over, the 1,500. Only this Polish man told us about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: John, was it unusual that the-- that the gypsies guarded a camp? Wasn't that unusual?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was unusual, because we-- we didn't even know they are gypsies for the first, because they were normal Germans to us. They were all German gypsies.

speaker: interviewer
text: They wore uniforms?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They were prisoners, but in their civilian clothes. And these gypsies were actually gassed and killed around September 1944. And when they took them onto tracks, that was a panic, because they knew exactly what's going on. And they were together with their children in-- in those little rooms. I wasn't there at time. I heard about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who-- who was still with you from the ghetto, or from-- from the time you left your home village? Anybody still with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. This [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] young man-- now he is not young anymore-- called Herschel Silberstein. He lives in [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Anybody else?

speaker: interviewee
text: And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Pearl Lazar. He lives in [NON-ENGLISH]. Now Pearl Lazar had also his father with him. He died in-- in Allach from weakness, starvation.

speaker: interviewer
text: The father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. he-- he was heavy smoker. And he gave his own bread away for cigarettes. And anyone who did that, three months, he was finished.

speaker: interviewer
text: Didn't have a chance.

speaker: interviewee
text: He didn't have a chance. And we were given cigarettes in-- in those camps in Allach. [NON-ENGLISH], Yugoslav cigarettes, 60 a month, two per day. I never ever sold cigarettes to fellow prisoners. Only to the guards, to the Germans. Germans didn't have enough cigarettes. They bought it. And they actually gave you more bread than the prisoners could give you. But the first few days, the price of bread was you had to give too-- too many cigarettes for a bread. If you waited a week, you could hide your cigarettes, then you made a much better deal. It was 12 cigarettes for a bread. In a week's time you-- you get bread for eight cigarettes, because the cigarette stock went down.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well where did you-- where could you hide it? You were wearing prison uniform.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Well in Allach, you could hide it

speaker: interviewer
text: That's afterwards

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, afterwards. In your bed, in your mat, under your mattress, or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: But in-- in Birkenau you couldn't hide it.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Birkenau nobody gave you cigarettes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you couldn't-- you had no pockets on your striped uniforms?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no pockets, no. And we were taken to the trains in-- in Birkenau from F Lager, and given that bread and a bit of food, and put into wagons, unload the wagons

speaker: interviewer
text: Still cattle cars.

speaker: interviewee
text: Cattle cars. But each car-- each car had two German soldiers with us-- guards--

speaker: interviewer
text: Inside?

speaker: interviewee
text: Inside. The German soldiers had straw. So they had a bit soft. And we didn't have straw. So I actually, maybe my father was next to that guard, because they occupied the center where the doors were. And he decided the door, they were only putting 40 people in, not 80. 20 on either side.

speaker: interviewee
text: So even I've got a bit of food from them on the way. I was too young, so they felt sorry for me. And they were old Germans, not SS guard. They were in their 50s.

speaker: interviewee
text: We came-- we went through Katowice, Gliwice. It's a double locomotive, very fast. And come through Vienna. [NON-ENGLISH] to Vienna, was, I remember Vienna got bombed in the night. When we came there in the morning it was smoldering, and all the station were half in ruins.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this--

speaker: interviewee
text: You could see fire brigades and pipes everywhere, water sprinkling. And we were only there because some tracks were still operational. So there-- there was some holdup. We were there about half an hour, no more. Then, off we went.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was-- when was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh that was probably-- couldn't give an exact date. But it was on the-- must have been beginning of June

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] '44 June. And we went-- we came to Dachau, not to the concentration camp, to the railway station, which is a small German station with three, four platforms. And 500 of us were cut off, disembark from there. And 1,000 off to concentration camp in New Dachau. And we were wait-- a German, there was an SS officer, a lieutenant, in whole uniform, leather jacket, leather long coat, and motorbike. I remember a 250cc BMW in German military colors.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't know his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. And-- and he had been a few guards there, and he had a short speech that I understand German a bit from what I learned in school. And he said, you're going to work in this place, and you'll be well fed. We already-- we thought we would be fed before killed, like--

speaker: interviewer
text: Fattened.

speaker: interviewee
text: --Like the fellows in the jungle, then eat us! Feed you before killing you. Then we went off. From there, the guards, 500, to-- we walked through nicely wheat fields. The wheat was still on the field, getting yellow slightly. And all this good smell of-- of wild flowers in the wheat field.

speaker: interviewee
text: We came to a camp about an hour's walk. There's no houses in the way. Nothing. And that camp, as we approached, we saw that it got no electric fences. Just ordinary wire fence, which-- what they use? How you call that wire? Barbed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Barbed wire. Thanks. And four towers, it was a small camp with four guard towers on the four-- thing. And on a gate also from barbed wired wooden gate. We came in the camp, and we had to discard the clothing. You were given fresh ones.

speaker: interviewer
text: Still striped clothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Striped clothing. After having a shower and get clothing, we get eight in a room. It was smaller rooms. And now-- we're now three-- three people on the one above you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Two bunks.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was eight bunks in the room. That's 16 people. And after that--

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father is still with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, with me. And after that, they told us we could come. We had a-- that was a square sort of a camp-- no barracks. Only the barracks were interconnecting with each other, and they had a kitchen, and a sort of a dining room. And we went-- they said to come to a dining room. We never had dining room. And they had benches there with long tables, and with sliced army bread in baskets. And there were-- we-- we could eat as much as we wanted. And they had this bunker zuppa very thick with meat. I was-- I had hungry for a few weeks. And that had a speech, of course, when we take as another three, four days before we were-- we were go to proper work. But I was-- in appeal, I was taken by this officer. He asked--

speaker: interviewer
text: The one who met you on the bus-- on the bike?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the bike. He-- he made the appeal.

speaker: interviewer
text: The parade. He is second.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the parade. And he just pulled me over, and told me to clean up his little office. His office was inside the camp. And-- and he had a shortwave radio, medium-wave and shortwave. I wouldn't dare listening to it. And the whole room was only about 2 by 3 meter, with an-- with an office desk, a telephone, and reading lamp. And he had the the Nazi party's paper, with the red print on top. And the [NON-ENGLISH] was the Munich German newspaper. And they already had the-- the invasion of France was 6th of June. Could have been a few days-- one or two days after that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So after I cleaned up, he told me where was the water, and the mop. Mopped up, emptied his paper bin. He gave me half a [NON-ENGLISH]-- that's a German butter-- and a piece of white bread. And off I went. Did you read the newspaper first?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just the headlines. I couldn't make it out.

speaker: interviewer
text: John, did you trust-- did you trust this whole scene? Here you had-- when you went to the dining room, you had as much bread as you wanted to. He gave you some butter. You had white bread. Were you not suspicious of all the-- your treatment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it-- it was complete change for the last three weeks, and the ghetto. So it's not that-- not our hope, we didn't go up. It's just we became a bit careless, like worryless. Forget worrying. Whatever. We won't survive. Whatever we can have now is good.

speaker: interviewer
text: You make the best of it, in other words.

speaker: interviewee
text: You make the best of it. And next morning we went on appeal. And there, this officer ask, he needs ten young fellows who have their fathers here. There were more than 10. So I didn't know to go or not. So my father was next to me on appeal, and he said, go. We didn't know, what can they do? They make sausages out of us or what?

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went out, and the rest was discharged. And they told us, you-- tomorrow morning, 7 o'clock you going to work in Dachau in the-- in the city, in the township, and no guards with you. If you run away, your father gets shot. So that was it. We-- we had a-- we had a very good working place on a food store of the Army in the third organization, which was housed in-- in the roofs of requisitioned roofs of a number of buildings, which were all about three floor high, and slightly sloping, so they had a-- a little two-foot high brick wall between one building ends and the next one. But the roof was one over the whole lot.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the goods arriving there were very bulky stuff-- sugar, and rice, and flour, and some goodies, too. But these were mainly in 50 kilo or-- or larger bags. And to take them up, we had a crane with a steel rope, and then a little electric hoist operated.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the outside of the--

speaker: interviewee
text: From the truck, truck moved in--

speaker: interviewer
text: Outside of the building.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had to move it up through a shaft into the roof section. And inside the roof, we had trolleys, because different material were in different places. And you couldn't move the trolley from one house to the next because of these two-foot brick walls. So you had to take another trolley and repack them maybe two or three times. Some goods were on the same place, which we were lucky. Of course the-- the manager told us you can eat cheese, sardines, anything open. But don't take anything into the camp. So we-- we had even wafers there. Military stuff. Lunch-- lunch hour, we-- we got-- we got 16 liters sent out from the camp, hot food. But we didn't even touch it while we here. We-- we didn't want to take it back to the camp, so they wouldn't find out that we eat all the goodies. We throw them in the rubbish, on the gutter down, the 16 liter. We took back the empty, that was insulated like a thermos, for 20 liter ones. And the work was real heavy. We-- we could-- we were there about 10 days, two weeks. And then they canceled us. We were weak-- too weak. They wanted stronger men. And they took stronger ones with guards. So we ran back to other groups.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went back to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Back to the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the camp called?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rotschweig.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rotschweig. Can you spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: R-O-T-H-S-- no, sorry, R-O-T-S-C-H-W-E-I-G. Rotschweig. That is called where the camp was. But we didn't see any locality or village under this name.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK, let's-- we're going to change the tape.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. That's the end of Tape 3 of the interview with John Ullman. It's the 23rd of July, 1995, and we're in Sydney, Australia.
